scores.question: section 138 negotiable instruments cheque dishonour
scores.answer:
Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, pertains to the dishonour of a cheque [E2, E1]. A case under this Section may arise when a cheque, such as one issued for loan repayment, is dishonoured [E2].

Upon the dishonour of a cheque, Clause (b) of the proviso to Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act mandates that the payee or the holder must make a demand for payment [E4]. This demand is to be made by giving written notice to the drawer of the cheque after its return as unpaid [E4]. Such notice must be given within the time prescribed under Clause (b) of the proviso to Section 138 [E4]. For instance, if a cheque was dishonoured on 2nd July 1996, a notice dated 16th July 1996 would be considered within the prescribed time [E4].

Proceedings under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act can lead to a petitioner being summoned to face trial [E5].

scores.question: anticipatory bail section 438 CrPC sushila aggarwal
scores.answer:
An individual already in custody cannot reasonably request anticipatory bail under Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC) [E1]. This is because a person in custody would be unable to comply with the condition of being available for interrogation, which is a requirement for anticipatory bail [E1].

Applications for anticipatory bail under Section 438 CrPC are typically filed before the High Court, as exemplified by cases such as *Rajesh Kumar Sharma vs C.B.I.* [E3] and *Punit Kumar Pal vs State Of U.P And 2 Others* [E5] before the Allahabad High Court.

The provided grounding sources do not contain any information regarding "Sushila Aggarwal" (not in corpus).

scores.question: default bail section 167 CrPC chargesheet
scores.answer:
Default bail under Section 167(2) of the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC) is a significant legal provision that allows a defendant to be granted bail if the prosecution fails to file a chargesheet within the stipulated time [E1].

Key aspects related to default bail and chargesheet under Section 167 CrPC include:
1.  **Time Period Calculation**: The legal significance of including or excluding the day of remand when calculating the time period for default bail under Section 167(2) CrPC affects whether a defendant is granted default bail [E1].
2.  **Filing of Chargesheet**: The filing of a chargesheet is considered sufficient compliance with the provisions of Section 167 [E3]. This was observed in the case of *MANJEET SINGH v. STATE OF HARYANA* [E3].
3.  **Consequence of Failure to Furnish Surety**: If an accused, having been granted bail under Section 167(2) CrPC, fails to furnish surety within the prescribed 90/60 days period, the bail order is deemed to have extinguished, and the accused can be detained in custody [E5].

The Supreme Court of India has addressed matters related to criminal jurisdiction in cases such as *RITU CHHABARIA v. UNION OF INDIA & ORS.* [E2] and *CENTRAL BUREAU OF INVESTIGATION v. KAPIL WADHAWAN & ANR.* [E6]. The Gujarat High Court also dealt with a criminal miscellaneous application in *JYOTIBEN VINODBHAI v. STATE OF GUJARAT* [E4].
